title: Types of Data
examTip: Examiners love questions asking you to evaluate whether a method produces quantitative or qualitative data — always link data type to the theoretical perspective (positivist/interpretivist) that prefers it.
introTips: Primary data = you collected it; secondary data = someone else collected it first.
Quantitative = numbers; qualitative = words and meanings.
Always evaluate data types — no method is perfect.
intro: Before a sociologist can say anything meaningful about society, they need to decide what kind of evidence to collect — and that choice shapes everything: what questions they can answer, whose voices get heard, and whether their findings will be trusted. The type of data a researcher gathers is not a neutral technical decision — it reflects assumptions about what sociology is for and how social reality can be known.

keyPoints.heading: Primary vs Secondary Data
keyPoints.content: Primary data is collected directly by the sociologist for their specific research project — it did not exist before the research began. Examples include questionnaires, interviews, and observation. Secondary data already exists, having been collected by someone else for a different purpose — examples include official statistics, historical documents, diaries, media content, and previous research. Primary data advantages: tailored exactly to the research question, up to date, the researcher controls quality. Disadvantages: time-consuming, expensive, access to respondents can be difficult. Secondary data advantages: quick and cheap to obtain, often large-scale, allows historical comparisons over long time periods. Disadvantages: not collected for your specific purpose, may be out of date, reliability and validity unknown — you can't be sure how it was collected.
writeBullets: Primary data — collected by the researcher for their specific study
Secondary data — collected by someone else for a different original purpose
Primary advantage — tailored to research question, researcher controls quality
Primary disadvantage — expensive and time-consuming to collect
Secondary advantage — cheap, large-scale, allows historical comparisons
Secondary disadvantage — may not fit the research question, reliability unknown
keyPoints.memTip: Primary = produced by you. Secondary = someone else's leftovers.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse primary/secondary with quantitative/qualitative — stress that these are separate distinctions.

keyPoints.heading: Quantitative vs Qualitative Data
keyPoints.content: Quantitative data is numerical — it can be counted and analysed statistically. Examples: crime statistics, exam results, answers to closed questionnaire questions (tick boxes, Likert scales). Gives breadth — large samples — and appears objective and scientific, allowing comparisons across groups and time. Favoured by positivists such as Auguste Comte (who founded sociology as a science in the 1830s) and Émile Durkheim, who used suicide statistics in his landmark study 'Suicide' (1897) to demonstrate that even the most personal act had social causes. Qualitative data is non-numerical — it consists of words, descriptions, meanings, and feelings. Examples: interview transcripts, observation field notes, personal diaries. Gives depth and insight into the meanings people attach to their experiences. Favoured by interpretivists such as Max Weber, who argued sociology must seek verstehen (empathetic understanding of meaning), and G.H. Mead, who developed symbolic interactionism — the idea that humans act on the basis of the meanings they give to situations.
writeBullets: Quantitative data — numerical, statistical, allows large-scale comparisons
Positivists (Comte, Durkheim) favour quantitative — sociology as a science
Qualitative data — words and meanings, gives depth and insight
Interpretivists (Weber, Mead) favour qualitative — understanding verstehen
Durkheim's 'Suicide' (1897) — classic positivist use of official statistics
Verstehen — Weber's term for empathetic understanding of social action
keyPoints.memTip: Quantitative = quantity (numbers). Qualitative = quality (meaning and depth).
keyPoints.tutor_note: Link data type to theoretical perspective — positivist prefers quantitative, interpretivist prefers qualitative. This is a high-mark connection.

keyPoints.heading: Official Statistics
keyPoints.content: Official statistics are numerical data collected by government departments and agencies. Examples: police-recorded crime statistics (published annually by the ONS), unemployment figures, education data from the Department for Education, NHS health data, and birth/death rates. Advantages: large-scale and comprehensive, free to access, allow identification of trends over long time periods, usually representative of the national population. Disadvantages: may not measure what they claim to measure — the classic example is the 'dark figure' of crime (crime that occurs but is never reported or recorded). Crime goes unrecorded because: victims don't report (embarrassment, fear, distrust of police), or police don't record (discretion, recording practices). Official statistics are collected for administrative purposes, not sociological ones — and can be politically manipulated (e.g., changing the definition of 'unemployment' to reduce the official count). They reflect the priorities and practices of those who collect them. Durkheim's 'Suicide' (1897) is the most famous use of official statistics in sociology — he argued that suicide rates varied systematically by religion and social integration, proving suicide had social not just psychological causes.
writeBullets: Official statistics — numerical data collected by government agencies (ONS, police, NHS, DfE)
Advantages — large-scale, free, allow historical trend analysis
Dark figure of crime — unrecorded crime that distorts official statistics
Collected for administrative not sociological purposes — may not fit research questions
Politically sensitive — definitions can be changed to improve government figures
Durkheim 'Suicide' (1897) — classic positivist use of official statistics
keyPoints.memTip: Official stats look scientific but hide what doesn't get counted — ask: what's missing?
keyPoints.tutor_note: The dark figure of crime is examinable in both S3 and S4 — make sure students can explain it clearly.

keyPoints.heading: Personal Documents and Other Secondary Sources
keyPoints.content: Personal documents include diaries, letters, and autobiographies — they provide rich qualitative insight into personal experience and historical periods. The classic sociological use is William I. Thomas and Florian Znaniecki's 'The Polish Peasant in Europe and America' (1919), which used letters and diaries exchanged between Polish migrants and their families to study the experience of migration — one of the first major works of American sociology. Newspapers and media reflect and shape social attitudes but carry significant biases (ownership bias, editorial line, commercial pressures). Historical documents allow study of past societies that can't be directly observed. Content analysis is the systematic study of media content — for example, counting how many women appear in TV advertisements, what roles they play, or how ethnic minorities are portrayed in news coverage. Advantages of personal documents and secondary sources: give access to past events, no cost to collect, can provide data impossible to gather any other way. Disadvantages: not representative (whose diaries survive?), researcher may impose their own interpretation on material, authenticity can be hard to verify.
writeBullets: Personal documents — diaries, letters, autobiographies — rich qualitative insight
Thomas and Znaniecki 'The Polish Peasant' (1919) — used letters to study migration
Newspapers and media — reflect social attitudes but have ownership and editorial bias
Content analysis — systematic counting and categorising of media content
Historical documents — allow study of societies that cannot be directly observed
Disadvantage — not representative; researcher's interpretation shapes findings
keyPoints.memTip: Personal documents give you a window into the past — but whose window, and is it clean?
keyPoints.tutor_note: Content analysis bridges quantitative and qualitative — it can be both. Worth noting for higher-mark evaluation questions.

tables.title: Types of Data: Comparison
rows: Primary
Questionnaires, interviews, observation
Tailored to research question; up to date; researcher controls quality
Expensive; time-consuming; access problems
All researchers — but essential when no suitable secondary source exists
Secondary
Official statistics, diaries, media, historical documents
Cheap; quick; large-scale; allows historical comparisons
Not designed for your purpose; may be outdated; reliability unknown
Positivists (large datasets); historians
Quantitative
Crime statistics, exam results, tick-box questionnaire answers
Large samples; objective-looking; allows statistical analysis and comparison
Lacks depth; may miss meanings and context
Positivists (Comte, Durkheim)
Qualitative
Interview transcripts, observation notes, diaries
Rich depth; captures meaning and lived experience; flexible
Small samples; hard to generalise; subjective analysis
Interpretivists (Weber, Mead)

1.question: Durkheim's 'Suicide' (1897) is a famous example of which type of data being used to make a sociological argument?
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Durkheim used government suicide statistics — secondary quantitative data — to show that suicide rates varied by religion and social integration, demonstrating social rather than individual causes.
1.wrongFeedback: Think again. Durkheim didn't interview anyone or observe groups — he worked at his desk with figures published by governments. What type of data is that?

3.question: Thomas and Znaniecki's 'The Polish Peasant in Europe and America' (1919) is an example of which type of secondary source?
3.correctFeedback: Correct. Thomas and Znaniecki analysed letters written between Polish migrants and their families — a classic use of personal documents as secondary qualitative data.
3.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Thomas and Znaniecki didn't use government data or conduct interviews — they worked with written materials produced by the migrants themselves. What category does that fall into?

commonMistakes: Confusing primary/secondary with quantitative/qualitative — these are separate distinctions. A questionnaire is primary; a government statistic is secondary. Both can be quantitative.
Thinking official statistics are always reliable — they have serious validity problems (dark figure of crime, politically manipulated definitions).
Saying qualitative data is 'better' or 'worse' — it depends on the research question. Depth vs breadth is the real trade-off.
Forgetting to name the theoretical perspective linked to each data type — positivists prefer quantitative; interpretivists prefer qualitative.
Describing content analysis as purely qualitative — it can be quantitative (counting appearances) or qualitative (analysing meaning and representation).
examTips: In evaluation questions, always link data type to a theoretical perspective — positivist/quantitative or interpretivist/qualitative — for higher marks.
When asked about official statistics, always mention the 'dark figure' of unrecorded crime as a validity problem.
For 4-mark or 6-mark questions on data types, structure your answer: define → example → advantage → disadvantage.
revisionCardBullets: Primary data — collected by the researcher for their own study (questionnaires, interviews, observation)
Secondary data — collected by someone else for a different purpose (official stats, diaries, media)
Quantitative — numerical data; allows statistical analysis; preferred by positivists (Comte, Durkheim)
Qualitative — words and meanings; gives depth; preferred by interpretivists (Weber, Mead)
Verstehen — Weber's concept of empathetic understanding of social meaning
Official statistics — government data; large-scale but suffer from 'dark figure' problem
Dark figure of crime — crime that happens but is never reported or recorded
Durkheim 'Suicide' (1897) — used official statistics to show social causes of suicide
Thomas and Znaniecki (1919) — used personal letters to study Polish migration
Content analysis — systematic study of media content (quantitative or qualitative)
Personal documents (diaries, letters) — rich qualitative data but not representative
Primary advantage: tailored and current; disadvantage: expensive and time-consuming
